[445] c_101v_02 This sentence appears to be unfinished. ↩
[446] c_102r_01 This appears to be an inverted mercury symbol (☿), deleted by the author-practitioner. ↩
[447] c_102r_02 According to Renaissance lexicographer Randle Cotgrave, ciment royal is "An excellent Cement, or Solder, used by Goldsmiths; (heretofore Mintmen compounded a Cement for the tryall of gold, and tearmed it so.)" See A Dictionarie of the French and English Tongues (London: Adam Islip, 1611), http://www.pbm.com/~lindahl/cotgrave. ↩
[448] c_102v_05 Obsolete term for the flower of rosemary. "Ce mot est Grec, et veut dire Fleur en general. Cependant tous les Apothicaires entendent par là la Fleur de Rosmarin, comme estant la plus considerable de toutes les fleurs, et meritant d'estre appellée Fleur par excellence;" Thomas Corneille, Le Dictionnaire Des Arts et Des Sciences (Paris: Vve J. B. Coignard, 1694) https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/bpt6k50507s. ↩
[449] c_102v_06 Meaning is unclear. It could refer to the first sediment of wine, or products from early in the wine-making season. ↩
[450] c_102v_03 Pope Paul III, born Alessandro Farnese (1468–1549), head of the Catholic Church from 1534 to his death. ↩
[451] c_102v_01 Correction in original: f- written over a- ↩
[452] c_114r_05 i.e., the Lord's Prayer ↩1 ↩2
[453] c_103r_01 Correction in original: t- written over p- ↩
[454] c_104v_04 This could refer to a garment such as a brigandine or coat of plates, also known as a jack. ↩
[455] c_105r_01 Referring to the drawings in the left margin, marked "B" ↩
[456] c_105v_03 The repetition of que in cases like this is a normal occurence in the sixteenth century. ↩
[457] c_105v_06 A grafting technique, possibly in which cuts are made in the shape of the blade of a small axe (piolet). ↩
[458] p120r_3 ↩
[459] c_105v_05 Armol is a plant in the amaranth family (Atriplex hortensis), close to the Spanish armuelle, and usually known in English as "orache." ↩
[460] c_105v_04 Nightingales are indeed discussed again in an entry fifteen folios further on from this one, beginning on fol. 120r. ↩
[461] c_106r_03 May be synonymous with spalt and spat used elsewhere in the manuscript. ↩
[462] c_106r_02 A mass of iron from the smelting furnace in a pasty condition, also known as a bloom. ↩
[463] c_106r_01 It is unclear whether the + symbol is associated with the titular insertion or the beginning of the marginal note. ↩
[464] c_106v_07 This may be a continuation of the discussion of plaster of Paris in the last paragraph of fol. 106r. ↩
[465] c_106v_02 Continuation of an entry from fol. 106v that ended with this same symbol. ↩1 ↩2
[466] c_106v_01 Correction in original: E- written over A- ↩
[467] c_106v_03 Insertion in the left margin continues down and across the bottom of the page, underneath the main body of the last entry. ↩
[468] c_106v_06 See corresponding symbol in the upper left margin on fol. 107r for continuation of this entry. ↩
[469] c_107r_03 It appears the author-practitioner confounded aseures and azures, striking out his first attempt to write the word, then writing aseures before subsequently adding azures. ↩
[470] c_107r_06 Reading pinon here as pilon ↩
[471] c_107v_03 Defined as ardille (clay) on fol. 112v. ↩
[472] c_107v_05 Correction in original: v over ut ↩
[473] c_107v_06 Hesitation over choice of Latin word. ↩
[474] c_107v_11 Latin: "A secret unknown to almost everyone in the art of foundry: the thing that needs to be molded, whether a plant or an animal such as a lizard, should be first immersed in previously rectified wine spirit, then sprinkled with compound powder, or else rubbed (if the powder has turned into a pulpy state, as often happens)." ↩
[475] c_107v_12 Correction in original: d- written over l- ↩
[476] c_108r_02 Spat or spalt is likely a German word referring to an earth used to make molds, which the author-practitioner reports is found mainly in Augsburg. ↩1 ↩2 ↩3 ↩4
[477] c_108r_01 Correction in original: d- written over p- ↩
[478] c_108r_03 Correction in original: E- written over M- ↩
[479] c_108v_02 This superscript addition continues in the left margin before the next line. ↩
[480] c_109r_01 i.e., the bottom or floor ↩
[481] c_109v_02 Satan: the devil in the Abrahamic religious traditions. ↩
[482] c_109v_01 What appear to be multiple additions around this entry may give clues to authorial intent and composition of the text. ↩
[483] c_110r_03 presumably days ↩
[484] c_110v_03 Reading Mays (But) as mects (put) ↩
[485] c_111r_01 Correction in original: -elle written over -il ↩
[486] c_111v_04 Compare this recipe for sal ammoniac water to that on fol. 118v. ↩
[487] c_111v_02 Correction in original: -o- written over -e- ↩1 ↩2
[488] c_111v_03 An insertion line from the main text indicates where the marginal comment is relevant. ↩
[489] c_111v_01 An insertion line above Si ton plastre indicates where in the main text the marginal comment is relevant. ↩
[490] c_111v_05 Correction in original: A- written over & ↩
[491] c_112r_01 It is unclear to which entry (or entries) this refers. ↩
[492] c_112r_04 Correction in original: en written over de ↩
[493] c_112r_05 Correction in original: a- written over d- ↩1 ↩2
[494] c_112r_06 Correction in original: -es written over -aulx ↩
[495] c_112v_04 Correction in original: -uy written over -es ↩
[496] c_112v_05 Correction in original: h- written over q- ↩
[497] c_112v_01 May illustrate the wire point being discussed. ↩
[498] c_112v_02 Vertical lines on either side of this maginal note separate it from both the previous note and the main text. ↩
[499] c_112v_03 See the corresponding symbol in the upper left margin on fol. 113r for continuation of this entry. ↩
[500] c_113r_01 Correction in original: en written over t- ↩
[501] c_128r_02 Correction in original: -e written over -il ↩1 ↩2 ↩3 ↩4 ↩5
[502] c_113r_04 Correction in original: le written over ce ↩
[503] c_113r_02 Refers to the figure three lines up, marked "b" ↩
[504] c_113r_05 Correction in original: s- written over v- ↩
[505] c_113r_03 Continuation of an entry from fol. 112v that ended with this same symbol. ↩
[506] c_113v_03 Correction in original: -en written over -il ↩
[507] c_113v_04 Correction in original: te written over de ↩
[508] c_113v_01 Meaning of marks here is uncertain. ↩
[509] c_113v_02 Meaning unclear: ne laissera pas (will not let) likely intended instead of ne sera pas (will not be) ↩
[510] c_114r_03 These two marks seem to indicate that the text of a corresponding marginal note (which begins with the first mark and ends with the second mark) should be inserted here. ↩1 ↩2
[511] c_114r_04 This marginal note is bracketed by two marks (one that precedes the text and one that follows it) which appear consecutively in the body text, indicating that the text of this note should be inserted in the main text where the two marks appear. ↩1 ↩2
[512] c_114v_01 See the corresponding symbol in the upper left margin on fol. 115r for continuation of this entry. ↩
[513] c_115r_01 Continuation of an entry from fol. 114v that ended with this same symbol. ↩
[514] c_115r_05 Correction in original: sable (sand) written over moule (mold) ↩
[515] c_115r_03 Correction in original: Et written over N- ↩
[516] c_115r_02 Correction in original: co- written over something illegible ↩
[517] c_115r_04 Correction in original: l- written over t- ↩
[518] c_115v_01 See the corresponding symbol in the upper left margin on fol. 116r for continuation of this entry. ↩
[519] c_116r_05 Correction in original: -e written over -ont ↩
[520] c_116r_01 Correction in original: -ansts written over -s- ↩
[521] c_116r_03 Continuation of an entry from fol. 115v that ended with this same symbol. ↩
[522] c_116r_02 Correction in original: -u written over -e ↩
[523] c_116r_04 Correction in original: -ou written over -ail ↩
[524] c_116r_06 Correction in original: -e written over -or ↩
[525] c_116r_07 Correction in original: -eu- written over -uy ↩
[526] c_116r_08 Correction in original: -f- written over -d- ↩
[527] c_116v_02 i.e., the sal ammoniac ↩
[528] c_116v_01 The fragment erem of the word particulierem[{ent}] (particularly) was written below the end of the previous line. ↩
[529] c_117r_02 presumably plain water ↩
[530] c_118r_01 Likely a variant of the German stüf, a kind of granular limestone or tufa. ↩
[531] c_118v_02 Correction in original: -s- written over -l- ↩
[532] c_118v_01 Compare this recipe for sal ammoniac water to that on fol. 111v. ↩
[533] c_118v_03 Correction in original: A- written over Ja ↩
[534] c_119r_01 Latin: "Note" ↩1 ↩2
[535] c_119v_04 Correction in original: le spat is written over la spalt ↩
[536] c_119v_02 Conrad Gessner, also known as Conradus Gesnerus (1516–1565): Swiss physician, natural historian, and bibliographer. ↩
[537] c_119v_03 Conrad Gessner, De Omni Rerum Fossilium Genere, Gemmis, Lapidibus, Metallis, et Huiusmodi, Libri Aliquot, Plerique Nunc Primum Editi (Zürich: Hans Jakob Geßner, 1566). ↩
[538] c_120r_03 Correction in original: R- written over Il ↩
[539] c_120r_02 Correction in original: -e written over -uy ↩
[540] c_120v_08 This entire entry and its marginal note in Latin (Siscitatio dubia) has been struck out with faint diagonal lines. A second marginal note may be an alternative set of instructions for this substance that will "make silver run." ↩
[541] c_120v_01 Correction in original: -e written over -u ↩
[542] c_120v_02 The possible change in ink color here and above (at cuivre menue (thin copper)) may be additions or corrections by the main hand at a different time. ↩
[543] c_120v_04 The subject (casting in silver) resumes on fol. 121v which is two pages ahead. ↩
[544] c_120v_07 Latin: "Doubtful question" ↩
[545] c_120v_03 Correction in original: d- written over v- ↩
[546] c_121r_03 A genus of flowering plant, comprising buttercups, spearworts, and crowfoots. ↩
[547] c_121r_02 A variety of buttercup (Caltha palustris) in the ranunculus family. ↩
[548] c_121r_01 Word uncertain. It may be partially obscured by the paper strip. It may be cos, coin, or otherwise. Context suggests it may refer to a vessel. ↩
[549] c_121v_01 This mark likely indicates the insertion point for the marginal comment about the proper mixing order of water and sand. ↩
[550] c_122r_02 i.e., roll the wax out on the table ↩
[551] c_123r_01 Italian: to make silver run [with the word argento (silver) written in reverse] ↩
[552] c_123v_01 Correction in original: -i written over -l ↩1 ↩2 ↩3
[553] c_124r_01 The plant dandelion is written here as dent de Lyon (literally, tooth of the lion), as if referring to the city (Lyon) instead of the animal (lion). ↩
[554] c_124v_02 The alignment of esmail and the possible change in ink may indicate a later addition. ↩
[555] c_124v_03 Correction in original: -i written over -u ↩
[556] c_124v_04 See the corresponding symbol in the upper left margin on fol. 125r for continuation of this entry. ↩
[557] c_125r_01 Continuation of an entry from fol. 124v that ended with this same symbol. ↩
[558] c_125r_03 Scimitars were not made in the way described here, but were instead made from steel and iron bars forge-welded in a pattern. The author-practitioner misunderstands what he sees as the traces of a sand casting process. ↩
[559] c_125r_04 The note is circled to offset it from the surrounding marginal text about plaster, and its placement suggests that it refers to the entry on scimitars. ↩
[560] c_125r_05 This is in the same script and centered location on the page as the headings, but has no entry content below it. It is unclear what it is referring to. It could be referring to other text blocks on the page, whether "Hearing from afar," "Scimitars," "Plaster," the marginal note about plaster/casting, or the entry on the next page "Vipers and snakes." It might also be a heading for an entry that was never written. ↩
[561] c_125r_06 i.e., molds for casting in sugar are made without a gate. ↩
[562] c_126v_01 Correction in original: -u written over -es ↩
[563] c_127v_02 Reading chaulx (quicklime) as chaults (hot) ↩
[564] c_128r_04 Correction in original: t- written over d- ↩1 ↩2
[565] c_128r_01 This likely refers to the silver flux discussed on fol. 123r–v, also described in terms of "grain." ↩
[566] c_158v_02 This entry describes a process for coloring lifecast plants and thus is a recipe for painting on metal. ↩1 ↩2
[567] c_129r_01 Nature (nature) likely refers here to genitals. ↩
[568] c_129r_02 The last two letters "-lx" are missing in the original. They appear to have been cut off when the original choirs of the manuscript were cut apart during rebinding. See Smith, "Making Ms. Fr. 640," https://edition640.makingandknowing.org/#/essays/ann_326_ie_19. ↩
[569] c_129v_02 The note is framed to offset it from the heading and body text. ↩
[570] c_130r_02 This addition is expanded upon in the left margin, where the author-practitioner illustrates the iron wire. ↩
[571] c_130r_03 This expands upon the addition about iron wire in the body text. ↩
[572] c_130v_02 Correction in original: br- written over mo- ↩
[573] c_130v_01 Feroit rien (would do nothing) is written in the left margin but only make sense as a continuation of the interlinear addition on the previous line. ↩
[574] c_130v_03 Correction in original: A written over la ↩
[575] c_131r_01 Correction in original: -ie- written over -ei- ↩
[576] c_131r_02 Correction in original: -on- written over -ir ↩
[577] c_131v_01 Correction in original: est written over ch- ↩
[578] c_132v_02 Correction in original: p- written over d- ↩1 ↩2
[579] c_132v_01 Correction in original: -uy written over -a ↩
[580] c_133r_01 Correction in original: -e- written over -s ↩1 ↩2
[581] c_133r_02 Correction in original: p- written over g- ↩
[582] c_133r_03 Correction in original: A- written over L- ↩
[583] c_133v_01 Correction in original: -e written over -s ↩
[584] c_133v_02 Correction in original: -e written over -se ↩
[585] c_134v_01 This sun symbol (curved rays) is slightly different from a similar sun symbol (straight rays) used to indicate "gold" throughout the manuscript. ↩
[586] c_135r_01 Couleur here refers not to "color" but to a flux or liquefying agent which makes molten metals flow more easily. From the verb couler (to flow). ↩1 ↩2 ↩3
[587] c_135r_02 Correction in original: -une written over -e l ↩
[588] c_135v_01 This sun symbol (curved rays) is slightly different from a similar sun symbol (straight rays) used to indicate "gold" throughout the manuscript, including in the main text of this entry. ↩
[589] c_135v_03 Correction in original: -ge- written over -ch- ↩
[590] c_135v_02 i.e., form a skin on its surface ↩
[591] c_135v_04 Correction in original: -u- written over -n- ↩
[592] c_136r_01 Original word is obscured by ink stain. ↩
[593] c_136r_03 The word could not be traced. It appears to refer to some kind of device to hold molds in place. ↩
[594] c_136r_02 Latin: "sun" [i.e., gold] ↩
[595] c_136r_04 Correction in original: Esm written over D ↩
[596] c_137r_02 A word seems to have been begun, then partially crossed out, leaving an unexplained "d" ↩
[597] c_137r_04 Unidentified substance that is added to molten metals to make them run more fluidly through a mold, and also causes them to change color (hence perhaps its name, literally "tinting oil") ↩
[598] c_137v_01 Correction in original: v- written over p- ↩
[599] c_139r_01 A seemingly accidental vertical stroke is drawn immediately after this word. ↩
[600] c_139r_02 A seemingly accidental vertical stroke is drawn through the last letter. It is not a deletion. ↩
[601] c_139r_03 Correction in original: C- written over l- ↩
[602] c_139r_04 Correction in original: -il written over -e ↩
[603] c_139r_05 Correction in original: -e written over -i ↩
[604] c_141r_01 The final word, which is under the paper strip, remains slightly uncertain. ↩
[605] c_141r_02 The last two letters "-bs" are missing in the original. They appear to have been cut off when the original choirs of the manuscript were cut apart during rebinding. See Smith, "Making Ms. Fr. 640," https://edition640.makingandknowing.org/#/essays/ann_326_ie_19. ↩
[606] c_156v_06 Correction in original: o- written over & ↩1 ↩2
[607] c_142r_03 Correction in original: -n written over -ut ↩
[608] c_142r_01 Smudged, but most likely cest (it is) ↩
[609] c_142r_02 Correction in original: -imes over something illegible ↩
[610] c_142v_02 This marginal note is squeezed in between the main text and the other marginal notes, and appears to be in a different ink. ↩
[611] c_142v_07 a type of mold ↩
[612] c_144v_02 The subject (molding turtles) resumes two folios later on fol. 146v. ↩
[613] c_145r_04 Correction in original: -e written over -un ↩
[614] c_145r_05 Latin: "Investigate" ↩
[615] c_145v_01 Correction in original: E- written over & ↩
[616] c_145v_02 Correction in original: S- written over & ↩
[617] c_146r_01 Correction in original: p- written over b- ↩
[618] c_146r_02 Correction in original: -scuelle written over -au en ↩
[619] c_146v_02 Two folios earlier (fol. 144v), the author-practitioner indicates that his discussion of this subject (molding turtles) resumes here. ↩
[620] c_147r_02 The subject (molding turtles), begun on fol. 144r and continued here, resumes again two folios later on fol. 148v. ↩
[621] c_147v_01 An unknown superscript abbreviation renders the meaning unclear. ↩1 ↩2
[622] c_147v_02 Correction in original: L- written over I- ↩
[623] c_148r_01 Appears to be an unfinished sentence. ↩
[624] c_148r_02 From this word on, the heading is written in the script of main text (i.e., not the Italic script used for headings), which may indicate a later addition. ↩
[625] c_148r_03 Change of ink here may give clues to authorial intent and composition of the text. ↩
[626] c_148v_01 Two folios earlier (fol. 147r), the author-practitioner indicates that his discussion of this subject (molding turtles), begun on fol. 144r and continued on 146v, resumes again here. ↩
[627] c_149v_02 Correction in original: f- written over g- ↩
[628] c_149v_01 This final sentence and the marginal note appear to be in a different ink, which may give clues to authorial intent and composition of the text. ↩
[629] c_150r_01 What appears to be a smudged capital letter to the left of this heading may indicate that the author-practitioner first began this entry there and then began the heading anew in the center. ↩
[630] c_150v_04 Correction in original: -s written over -r ↩
[631] c_150v_03 i.e., the first mold made directly from the turtle (see fol. 146v) and therefore the one closest to its natural appearance. ↩
[632] c_150v_05 Correction in original: -i- written over -a- ↩
[633] c_150v_02 This note continues on the next page (fol. 151r) in the bottom left margin at the letter "A" ↩
[634] c_151r_02 Master Alexander, an unidentified artisan ↩
[635] c_151r_01 This note begins on the previous page (fol. 150v) in the bottom left corner and ends in the bottom right with the letter "A" ↩
[636] c_151v_02 The first "r" in grommeleures is followed by a seemingly accidental vertical stroke. Meaning unclear. ↩
[637] c_151v_01 Likely the same as gouges (gouges). See glossary. ↩
[638] c_152r_01 Dionysius I of Syracuse (ca. 432–367 BCE): Greek tyrant who burned off his beard rather than trust a barber to trim it for fear the barber would cut his throat. ↩
[639] c_152r_02 This and surrounding words smudged but legible ↩
[640] c_152r_03 The final letter "-s" is missing in the original. It appears to have been cut off when the original choirs of the manuscript were cut apart during rebinding. See Smith, "Making Ms. Fr. 640," https://edition640.makingandknowing.org/#/essays/ann_326_ie_19. ↩
[641] c_152v_02 bequet: literally "a small beak." A tool used in metalworking, likely a type of vise attached to a goldsmith's table. ↩
[642] c_153r_03 The principal will not melt if used with molten tin or lead because it is made of metal, a copper alloy with a higher melting point. ↩
[643] c_153r_01 The marginal note appears to be in a darker ink, which may give clues to authorial intent and composition of the text. ↩
[644] c_153r_02 Word obscured by inkblot ↩
[645] c_154r_01 All notes in the left margin appear to be in a darker ink, which may give clues to authorial intent and composition of the text. ↩
[646] c_154r_02 & letton (latten) partially obscured by inkblot but legible ↩
[647] c_154v_01 Both additions in this entry, the one here in the heading and the other in the main text, appear to be in a darker ink, perhaps suggesting they were written together at a later time. ↩
[648] c_156r_01 This sentence makes more sense for the process being described by reading the "or" (ou) as an "and" (et). ↩
[649] c_156v_03 Correction in original: -g written over -e ↩
[650] c_156v_07 lanugineux (downy) is written in two parts at the right margin, lanugi above and neux below ↩
[651] c_156v_04 Correction in original: d- written over u- ↩
[652] c_156v_05 Correction in original: -e written over -a ↩
[653] c_156v_02 See figure on fol. 165v, which illustrates these instructions. ↩
[654] c_157r_04 Masselote (mod. machelotte), from machelote, an "excess reserve of liquid metal for filling the mold," according to TLFi: Trésor de la Langue Française informatisé (Nancy: ATILF - CNRS & Université de Lorraine, 1994), http://www.atilf.fr/tlfi. By 1704, the word is used to designate "superfluous metal that remains in the mold after casting a cannon." ↩
[655] c_157v_04 lester (weighting) here seems to mean weighing down the feathers, likely with the adoub (dressing) mentioned in the next sentence, to prevent them from falling out or being disturbed in the molding process. ↩
[656] c_157v_02 This entry is added across the entire bottom of the page in a different, super-cursive hand (hand A', see also fol. 2v). ↩
[657] c_157v_01 This heading includes multiple words with abbreviation marks. The expanded content for two words remains unclear. ↩
[658] c_157v_03 Correction in original: -t written over -d ↩
[659] c_158r_02 The darker ink of this marginal note may give clues about authorial intent and composition of text. ↩
[660] c_158v_01 In three places on this folio (here, in the margin, and at the end of the last entry), there are changes in ink color and letter size. This may indicate later additions and provide clues to authorial intent and composition of text. ↩
[661] c_158v_03 Such a press is illustrated on fol. 160r. ↩
[662] c_159r_01 Likely adding eau-de-vie ↩
[663] c_159v_02 This partial drawing may have been a first attempt to draw the large press illustrated on the facing page, fol. 160r. ↩
[664] c_160r_01 Etrier (stirrup) refers to the metal bracket which is attached to the bottom of the screw and fixed to the upper plate of the press. ↩
[665] c_160r_02 i.e., the X-shaped cross named after the instrument of martyrdom of St. Andrew, a Christian Apostle. ↩
[666] c_160v_03 This mark appears to indicate that the text of the marginal note should be inserted here. This mark and the corresponding marginal note appear to be in a lighter ink, which may indicate a later addition. ↩
[667] c_160v_04 Correction in original: Et written over il ↩
[668] c_161r_03 This entry was written in various inks and hands. This may indicate later additions and provide clues to authorial intent and composition of text. ↩
[669] c_161r_01 Latin: "bricks." Gip de lateribus likely refers to a kind of brick mortar. ↩
[670] c_161r_02 The Latin nominative is alumen jamenum. A type of alum. ↩
[671] p088v_1 ↩
[672] c_161r_04 Latin: "Note." See corresponding marginal note that begins with the same mark and word, which are both written in a super-cursive hand, likely the author-practitioner's own in speed-writing mode (A', less cursive here than on fols. 2v and 157v). He seems to have initially placed this intervention in the previous sentence, then changed his mind. ↩
[673] c_161r_05 Latin: "Note." See corresponding addition in the main text that begins with the same mark and word. ↩
[674] c_161v_02 Roration (wettings) is a Latinism used to mean moisten. ↩
[675] c_162r_08 Clio is also the name of Book I of the Histories of Herodotus. In Greek mythology, Clio was the muse of history. ↩
[676] c_162r_01 A likely source is the 1566 Latin edition of Herodotus's Histories. See Herodotus, Herodoti Halicarnassei Historiæ Lib. IX. Et de Vita Homeri Libellus, ed. Lorenzo Valla and Henri Estienne (Paris: Henri Estienne; Ulrich Fugger, 1566), 1. ↩
[677] c_162r_02 Page references suggest a likely source is the 1566 Latin edition of Herodotus's Histories. See Herodotus, Herodoti Halicarnassei Historiæ Lib. IX. Et de Vita Homeri Libellus, ed. Lorenzo Valla and Henri Estienne (Paris: Henri Estienne; Ulrich Fugger, 1566), 1. Medea: in Greek mythology, daughter of King Aeëtes of Colchis. ↩
[678] c_162r_11 Gyges of Lydia (7th c. BCE): King of Lydia and founder of the Mermnad dynasty. ↩
[679] c_162r_04 Page references suggest a likely source is the 1566 Latin edition of Herodotus's Histories. See Herodotus, Herodoti Halicarnassei Historiæ Lib. IX. Et de Vita Homeri Libellus, ed. Lorenzo Valla and Henri Estienne (Paris: Henri Estienne; Ulrich Fugger, 1566), 3. ↩
[680] c_162r_03 Latin: "Six gold bowls weighing 30 talents." ↩
[681] c_162r_07 Alyattes of Lydia (ca. 610–560 BCE): King of Lydia and father of Croesus. ↩
[682] c_162r_09 Croesus (595–ca. 546 BCE): King of Lydia and son of Alyattes. ↩
[683] c_162r_10 Glaucus of Chios (8th c. BCE): Greek sculptor, often referred to as the inventor of the arts of soldering metals. ↩
[684] c_162r_05 Latin: "Bowl of welded iron at Delphi, a notable sight among all the offerings that are at Delphi, the work of Glaucus of Chios, who alone among all discovered iron welding." ↩
[685] c_162r_06 Latin: "welded iron" ↩
[686] c_163r_03 Reading pinon as pignon (pine nut) ↩
[687] c_164v_03 i.e., like a mitten ↩
[688] c_165v_02 This addition appears to be in a darker ink and begins as the last line of the main text before continuing into the right margin. This may indicate that it was added at a later time. ↩
[689] c_165v_03 This appears to be in a darker ink, which may indicate a later addition. ↩
[690] c_165v_04 i.e., the common lute ↩
[691] c_166r_01 This aphorism also appears, slightly rephrased, on fol. 1r. ↩
[692] c_166r_03 Numenius Numenius (2nd c. CE): platonist philosopher; see Ambrosius Aurelius Theodosius Macrobius and Arnoldus Haldrenius Vesaliensis, In Somnium Scipionis, Libri Ii. Saturnaliorum, Libri Vii. Ex Variis, Ac Vetustissimis Codicibus Recogniti et Aucti (Lyon: Gryphe, Sébastien, 1556), 1.2.19. Mentioned by various sixteenth-century authors, such as Juan Luis Vives, Los Comentarios de Juan Luis Vives a "La Ciudad de Dios" de San Agustı́n, ed. Rafael Cabrera Petit, vol. 6, Colección J.l. Vives (Ajuntament de Valencia, 2000); Vincenzo Cartari, Les Images Des Dieux, ed. Antoine du Verdier (Lyon: Guichard Julliéron, 1584), 276. The author-practitioner's knowledge that Numenius was a Pythagorean comes from elsewhere. ↩
[693] c_166r_02 A similar note appears in Latin on fol. 1r. Here, the author-practitioner offers an expanded French paraphrase. ↩
[694] c_166r_04 Titus Flavius Josephus, also known as Yosef ben Matityahu (1st c. CE): Jewish priest, soldier, and historian based in Rome. The quotation in Josephus has not been identified. See Flavius Josephus, De Antiquitate Judaica. De Bello Judaico, ed. Rufinus Aquileiensis and Girolamo Squarciafico (Venice: Vercellensis, Johannes Rubeus, 1486). ↩
[695] c_166r_12 Cebes of Thebes (ca. 430–350 BCE): Greek philosopher, disciple of Socrates, and author of the Table of Cebes. ↩
[696] c_166r_10 The word zelotype rarely occurs in French texts, although it does appear in Randle Cotgrave, A Dictionarie of the French and English Tongues (London: Adam Islip, 1611), http://www.pbm.com/~lindahl/cotgrave and “Dictionnaire Du Moyen Français.” Paris, 2015. http://www.atilf.fr/dmf. ↩
[697] c_166r_06 Latin: "Nothing is said now that has not been said or done before." This would seem to be glossing Terence (second century BCE), Eunuchus, Prologue: "Nothing is said now that has not been said before." The author here adds aut factum (or done), perhaps because his book is of deeds not words. ↩
[698] c_166r_13 Numa Pompilius (753–673 BCE): second King of Rome and religious founder. ↩
[699] c_166r_14 Marcus Tullius Cicero (106–43 BCE): Roman statesman, philosopher, and orator. ↩
[700] c_166r_15 Plato (ca. 428–ca. 447 BCE): Greek philosopher. ↩
[701] c_166r_16 Publius Vergilius Maro, also known as Virgil (70–19 BCE): Roman poet under the reign of Augustus. ↩
[702] c_166r_17 Homer (8th c. BCE): Greek poet. ↩
[703] c_166r_20 Titus Livius (64 or 59 BCE–12 or 17 CE): Roman historian. See Titus Livius, Historiae Romanae Ab Urbe Condita, Libri 15. Quotquot Ad Nostram Aetatem Peruenerunt, Cum Commentariis Omnium Interpretum Ad Explicationem Locorum Difficilium, E Regione Insertis (Paris: Jean Charron; Michel Sonnius, 1573) and Les Decades, ed. Blaise de Vigenère, Jean Hamelin, and Antoine de La Faye (Paris: Jacques du Puys, 1583). ↩
[704] c_166r_21 Polybius (208–126 BCE): statesman and historian from the Hellenistic period. See Polybius, Eklogai peri tōn presveiōn. Ex libris Polybii selecta de legationibus et alia quae seguenti pagina indicantur nunc primum in lucem edita, (Antwerp: Christophe Plantin, 1582). ↩
[705] c_166r_22 Serapis: Graeco-Egyptian sun god associated with healing and fertility. ↩
[706] c_166r_23 Ceres, also known as Demeter: Graeco-Roman goddess of harvest and agriculture. ↩
[707] c_166r_05 Pythagoras of Samos (ca. 570–ca. 495 BCE): Greek philosopher. ↩
[708] c_166r_24 Apollo: Graeco-Roman god associated with the sun, healing, knowledge, and various arts. ↩
[709] c_166r_25 Chirone: in Greek mythology, a Centaur famous for his wisdom and knowledge of medicine. ↩
[710] c_166r_08 Latin: "Apollo learned to medicate from Chiron, yet he is considered the god of medicine." Source unknown. This may be common knowledge of mythology written in the author-practitioner's own Latin. NB: Apollo put Chiron in charge of Asclepius's medical education. ↩
[711] c_166r_26 Orpheus: in Greek mythology, a hero endowed with superhuman musical skills. ↩
[712] c_166r_28 Peleus: in Greek mythology, hero and King of Phthia, and father of Achilles. ↩
[713] c_166r_29 Achilles: in Greek mythology, hero of the Trojan War and protagonist of Homer's Illiad. ↩
[714] c_166r_30 Justin Martyr (ca. 100–165 CE): Christian apologist and philosopher. On the editions of Justin Martyr, cf. André Wartelle, Bibliographie Historique et Critique de Saint Justin, Philosophe et Martyr, 1494-1994 (Avec Un Supplément) (Paris: Editions F. Lanore, 2001); Justinus Martyr, Zēna Kai Serēno. Logos Parainetikos Pros Ellēnas Pros Tryphōna Ioudaiōn Dialogos. Apologia Ypo Christianōn Pros Tēn Rōmaiōn Sygklēto. Apologia B' Ypo Christianōn Pros Antōninon Ton Eusebē (Paris: Robert Estienne, 1551); Opera Non Ita Pridem Graece Edita, Nuper Verò Latinè Reddita (Basel: Hieronymus Froben; Nikolaus Episcopius, 1555). ↩
[715] c_166r_09 Latin: "Homer imitated the poem of Orpheus, for, as Orpheus had begun, 'Iram cane, dea, Cereris frugiferentis,' so Homer repeated, 'Iram cane, dea, Pelidae Achillis.' In Justin Martyr, towards the beginning." Cf. Justinus Martyr, Opera Non Ita Pridem Graece Edita, Nuper Verò Latinè Reddita (Basel: Hieronymus Froben; Nikolaus Episcopius, 1555), 20. ↩
[716] c_166v_02 David (10th c. BCE): biblical figure, King of Israel. ↩
[717] c_166v_08 Athanasius of Alexandria (ca. 296–ca. 373 CE): theologian and statesman based in Egypt. ↩
[718] c_166v_07 Latin: "in the Synopsis" ↩
[719] c_166v_04 Latin: "I was small." See Bible, Ps. 151:1. ↩
[720] c_166v_05 Latin: "My hands made an organ and my fingers fashioned a psaltery." See Bible, Ps. 151:2. ↩
[721] c_166v_03 Uncertain reading ↩
[722] c_166v_06 Latin: "dead head," i.e., residue from an alchemical operation. The term often referred to metal oxides used as pigments, such as hematite (a red/purple iron oxide). ↩
[723] c_167r_01 A description of each tool depicted is given on fol. 168r–v. ↩
[724] c_167v_01 Fine powder used for priming a piece of artillery. ↩
[725] c_168v_01 i.e., a flange to hang it up ↩1 ↩2
[726] c_169v_02 Likely Istrian stone (pietra d'Istria), a building stone from the Republic of Venice or, perhaps, a stone from Istres, France. ↩
[727] c_169v_01 Correction in original: -s- written over -b- ↩
[728] c_170r_01 This marginal note is oriented 90 degrees clockwise to the main text and continues the last entry on this page which ends with a corresponding mark. ↩
[729] c_170r_03 Trampe is translated here as "tempered" but could also be "quenched." ↩
[730] c_170r_02 This continues in the margin indicated by a corresponding mark. ↩
[731] c_170v_12 This whole page is upside down. The first half of this page (now found at the bottom) is in a different hand (hand E, semi-calligraphic) and records payments of rent to the heirs of Sieur Ouvrier. It is struck through with diagonal lines and is separated from the subsequent text by a horizontal line. The second half of the page (now at the top), is in the author-practitioner's main hand (hand A). This suggests that he took a bound, mostly-blank book—perhaps a book recording accounts such as the ones found here—struck through the account records, and began to record medical recipes and a list of titles under them. Then, when he ran out of room, he flipped the entire book over, turned it upside down, and began writing on what now is the first folio of the manuscript (fol. 1r). ↩
[732] c_170v_13 The Ouvrier family (sometimes d’Ouvrier) was a prominent family in Toulouse (late 15th–18th c.), with some of its members serving in the city Parliament. Benoît Ouvrier (d. 1573), a merchant who owned a house located on the “rue des Fleurs,” near the Mint, is probably the feu sr Ouvryer mentioned here. The Ouvrier family had ties to the Du Cros family (see fol. 9v) through two marriages, one between Jean Ouvrier and Anne Du Cros in 1586, and the other between Gabriel Ouvrier (his brother) and Françoise Du Cros in 1592. See Debuiche and Muñoz, "Le Ms. Fr. 640 et le contexte toulousain," https://edition640.makingandknowing.org/#/essays/ann_312_ie_19. ↩
[733] c_170v_14 Saint-Frajou: Haute-Garonne, district of Saint-Gaudens, canton of Isle-en-Dodon (approx. 65 km SW of Toulouse) ↩
[734] c_170v_03 The meaning of this mark, visible under the paper strip in the right margin when the page is back-lit, is uncertain. It may be the symbol for the livre, though this currency is already mentioned in the same line. It may also be a hash-like mark indicating that the writer (hand E, semi-calligraphic) intended to continue this thought elsewhere on the page but forgot. ↩
[735] c_170v_15 Scabieuse (scabious) seems to refer to several kinds of plants, not all of which belong to the genus Scabiosa in modern botany. ↩
[736] c_170v_30 Otto I of Freising, also known as Otto Frisingensis (ca. 1114–1158): Bishop of Freising and chronicler. ↩
[737] c_170v_25 A twelfth-century chronicle: Otto Frisingensis and Benedictus Chelidonius, Rerun ab origine mundi ad ipsius usque tempora gestarum, libri octo. Eiusdem de gestis friderici primi aenobarbi caes.aug. Libri duo. Radevici phrisingen. Ecclesie canonici libri duo, prioribus additi, de eiusd friderici imp. Gestis (Strasbourg: Matthias Schürer, 1515); Otto Frisingensis et al., De eiudem frid. Gestis libri ii de gestis friderici i. Caes. Aug. Libri duo libri duo de eiusdem friderici imp. Gestis ligurini de gestis imp. Caesaris friderici primi augusti libri decem ligurinus, sive de gestis friderici, libri x. Ottonis episcopi frisingensis leopoldi pii marchionis austriae f.chronicon, sive rerum ab orbe condito ad sua usque tempora gestarum, libri octo. Eiusdem de gestis friderici i.caes.aug.libri duo. Radeuici frising.canonici de eiusdem frid. Gestis libri ii. Priorib.additi. Guntheri poëtae ligurinus, sive de gestis friderici, libri x. Addita sunt et alia, cum ad friderici, tum ad posteriorum imperatorum historiam pertinentia, adiectis etiam notis et indice accurato (Basel: Peter Perna, 1569). The reference here points to the 1569 edition. ↩
[738] c_170v_16 Latin: "Vinegar made from rue, juniper berries crushed together. With this vinegar red-hot bricks shall be extinguished and the vapor shall be received through the mouth & nostrils." ↩
[739] c_170v_17 Aer infaict (infected air) connotes a tiny amount of morbid substance spreading throughout the air, analogous to dye tinging clear water. ↩
[740] c_170v_19 Perhaps referring to seeds of the juniper berries mentioned in the Latin passage above. ↩
[741] c_170v_31 Conrad of Licthenau (d. 1240): German nobleman and provost of Ursberg Abbey. He is thought to be the author of the Chronicon urspergense. ↩
[742] c_170v_26 Burchard of Ursperg, Chronicum abbatis urspergensis, a nino rege assyriorum magno, usque ad fridericum ii.romanorum imperatorem, ex optimis autoribus recognitum, et innumeris mendis repurgatum (Strasbourg: Kraft Müller, 1537). ↩
[743] c_170v_32 Girolamo Mercuriale (1530–1606): physician from Forlì and professor of medicine in Padua, Bologna, and Pisa. ↩
[744] c_170v_27 Girolamo Mercuriale, Liber responsorum et consultationum medicinalium (Basel: Konrad von Waldkirch, 1588). ↩
[745] c_170v_04 This list of six titles in Italic script seems to be continued on fol. 1r with the singular Italic title, Aquatilium animalium historiæ, found near the bottom of the page. This might indicate that when the author-practitioner ran out of room here, he turned the entire bound text over and upside down to start writing on what is now the first folio of the manuscript. ↩
[746] c_170v_20 George Buchanan, Rerum Scoticarum Historia Auctore Georgio Buchanano Scoto (Edinburgh: Arbuthnet, Alexander, 1582). ↩
[747] c_170v_33 George Buchanan (1506–1582): Scottish historian and humanist. ↩
[748] c_170v_34 Rembert Dodoens, also known as Rembertus Dodonaeus (1517–1585): physician and botanist from Mechelen. ↩
[749] c_170v_21 Rembert Dodoens, Trium Priorum de Stirpium Historia Commentariorum Imagines Ad Vivum Expressae Una Cum Indicibus, Graece, Latine, Officinarum, Germanica, Brabantica Gallicaque Nomina Complectentibus (Antwerp: Loe, Jan van der, 1553). ↩
[750] c_170v_35 Philibert de l’Orme, also known as Delorme (1514–1570): French architect. ↩
[751] c_170v_22 Philibert Delorme, Nouvelles Inventions Pour Bien Bastir et a Petits Fraiz (Paris: Morel, Fédéric, 1561). ↩
[752] c_170v_36 Bernardino Telesio, also known as Bernardinus Telesius (1509–1588): Italian philosopher and natural scientist. ↩
[753] c_170v_23 Michel de Vascosan (ca. 1500–1576): Parisian printer. He is the publisher of Simone Porzio, De coloribus libellus, Latinitate donatus, et commentariis illustratus una cum praefatione, qua coloris naturam declarat (Paris: Michel de Vascosan, 1549), which the author-practitioner seems to confuse with Antonio Telesio, Libellus de Coloribus. Ubi Multa Leguntur Praeter Aliorum Opinionem (Venice: Vitali, Bernardino, 1528). Telesius is often associated (i.e., bound, printed) with works of French humanist Lazare de Baïf (1496–1547). See Franck Collin, "Antonio Telesio de Cosenza, Petit Traité Des Couleurs Latines (de Coloribus Libellus), éd. Michel Indergand et Christine Viglino," Cahiers de recherches médiévales et humanistes, 2010. ↩
[754] c_170v_37 Marbodius of Rennes, also known as Marbode and Marbœuf (1040–1123): Bishop of Rennes. ↩
[755] c_170v_24 Marbodus Redonensis, Libellus de Lapidibus Preciosis (Vienna: Vietor, Hieronymus, 1511). This work was reprinted frequently throughout the sixteenth century. ↩
[756] c_170v_38 Albertus Magnus, also known as Albert of Cologne and St. Albert (ca. 1200–1280): Dominican friar, bishop, theologian, and philosopher. ↩
[757] c_170v_28 Albertus Magnus, De mineralibus (Venice: Johannes de Gregoriis and Gregorius de Gregoriis, 1495). ↩

Tricks and sleight of hand

  • Cousteau pour couper le nes ou un doict (fol. 33r)
  • X Pour rallumer une chandelle estainte entre les mains sans soufler (fol. 33r)
  • Pour fayre sembler que à quelqu'un qu'il aura une piece d'argent au front (fol. 33v)
  • Pour fayre sortir du front de quelqu'un du sang ou du vin, ou d'une muraille (fol. 33v)
  • Pour dire à quelqu'un que, mettant tenant le doit tousjours au front, tu l'empescheras de sortir d'une chambre (fol. 34r)
  • X Gaiger a quelque'un que se promenant jusques à tel lieu et en revenir, il ne dira point quattre fois de suite botte sans esperon (fol. 34r)
  • Faire tenir un chandellier contre la muraille sans y fayre trou (fol. 34r)
  • X Ne casser point un verre d'une busche ou d'un gros baston (fol. 34r)
  • Escripre subtillement (fol. 34r)
  • Pour fayre fondre ou transmuer une bague mise dans une boitte (fol. 34v)
  • Pour dire a quelqu'un que tu luy monstreras enseigneras une chose qu'il ne sçait pas ne toy aussy (fol. 35r)
  • Pour fayre soubstenir un seau d'eau sur la poincte couchée de trois cousteaulx sans toucher à terre (fol. 35r)
  • Pour faire bouillir sans foeu dans de l'eau froide un oeuf (fol. 35r)
  • Pour faire sortir les connils d'un terrier (fol. 35r)
  • Pour garder quelqu'un de ne manger d'une viande (fol. 35v)
  • Pour fayre transmuer du bled d'un vaisseau à aultre (fol. 35v–36r)
  • Pour fayre transmuer une boule en un dé à couldre, subtil (fol. 36r)

Sensory

  • Ceste cire est fort doulce & amiable & pliante comme cuivre, et si est forte à cause du soufre qui la rend beaucoup plustost fondante que l'aultre, ainsy que tu en peulx faire preuve sur une ardoise chaulde 139v
  • chaudelet, de telle sorte que tu le puisse tenir à la main 115v
  • cognoissoit sur l'ongle 132v
  • comme ilz sont encores en telle chaleur que tu as cy devant remarqué mectant le doigt au trou, gecte 164v

Index of Essays

Research essays from the online edition, by the manuscript entry they discuss. Essay titles link to the edition; entry headings link to the entry in this document.
